narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 47% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for admission. This prediction is based on your academic record, including being in the 4th decile for Year 1 grades—below the typical student's 6th decile—and a much lower 1st decile for Year 3, compared to the average 6th decile there. Your LSAT score of 27 is well below the dataset average of about 37, and your undergraduate GPA of 2.6 falls short of the usual 3.2. As a female Native American student from a lower-middle income family planning to study part-time, your profile also differs from the majority, who are mostly full-time white males from higher income brackets. The model's decision was most heavily influenced by your Year 3 decile rank, which strongly pushed the prediction toward failure due to how low it is relative to peers. Next in influence was your LSAT score, again driving the risk higher because of its below-average level. Your Year 1 decile came third, similarly contributing to the failure prediction with its subpar standing. Factors like your racial background as Native American, lower-middle family income, undergraduate GPA, part-time study plans, and gender followed in descending order of impact, each nudging the odds toward not passing the bar. While all these elements played a role in tipping the scales against you, your later academic performance and standardized test results stood out as the biggest concerns. To improve your future chances, focus on strengthening your grades through dedicated study habits or tutoring, as school deciles are areas you can still influence before reapplying. Retaking the LSAT to aim above the average could make a substantial difference, since it's a key controllable factor. Building a stronger overall academic trajectory, perhaps by pursuing full-time enrollment if feasible, might also help align your profile closer to successful students. With targeted improvements, you could shift this prediction in your favor—keep pushing forward, as your determination will be key.
